VAL-ALLOC-001.details: heap.rs: 12008 bytes, linked-list first-fit allocator with slab routing. AUDIT comment is comprehensive, covering OOM handling, dealloc routing invariant, and 6-category checklist. No bugs found during audit.

VAL-ALLOC-005.evidence: OOM handling documented in heap.rs AUDIT comment (lines 5-17): Lists 3 allocation paths: (1) alloc() returns null on exhaustion (correct per GlobalAlloc contract), (2) dealloc() panics on out-of-region pointer (appropriate — bogus free is kernel bug), (3) Rust's handle_alloc_error panics on null from alloc(). Documents that all user-controlled paths (process_create, thread_create, channel_create, etc.) panic on OOM. Justifies: kernel heap (16 MiB) stores only kernel objects; OOM means kernel exhausted fixed pool — fatal with no safe recovery. Notes try_alloc refactor deferred to future hardening pass. slab.rs AUDIT comment (line 5) documents: 'OOM in grow() returns early, alloc() returns null, heap falls through to linked-list.' page_allocator.rs: alloc returns Option<Pa> (None on OOM). OOM fault injection available via set_fail_after().
VAL-ALLOC-005.details: OOM documentation is comprehensive and present in the AUDIT comments of the audited files. Each allocation path's OOM behavior is documented with disposition and justification for panic paths.

VAL-ALLOC-006.evidence: test/tests/heap_routing.rs contains 2 dedicated tests: (1) dealloc_routes_heap_pointers_to_linked_list_not_slab — allocates sizes matching slab classes (64, 128, 256, 512) with a greedy slab stub, verifies all frees go to linked-list not slab (slab::free_call_count() == 0). Uses address-based routing via is_in_heap_region(). (2) no_corruption_after_alloc_free_cycle — verifies alloc→free→realloc works without corruption when greedy slab stub is present. Both tests pass: 'test result: ok. 2 passed; 0 failed'. Tests verify the DESIGN.md §0.5 routing invariant: dealloc routes by address range, not size.
VAL-ALLOC-006.details: The test design is excellent: uses a greedy slab stub that accepts ANY free by size class, proving the heap's address-based routing prevents cross-allocator contamination. This directly validates the invariant.

VAL-ALLOC-007.evidence: test/tests/buddy.rs contains pa_validation_on_free test that models the kernel's PA validation logic (behind #[cfg(not(test))] in kernel code). Tests: misaligned PAs (off-by-1, half-page, one-below-page), PAs below RAM range (zero, one page below RAM_START), PAs above RAM range (RAM_END, one page past, high address), valid PAs (RAM_START, one page in, last valid page, middle of RAM), boundary alignment checks. Test passes: 'test pa_validation_on_free ... ok'. The kernel code in page_allocator.rs free_frames() (not(test) block) validates PA is page-aligned (pa.0 & 0xFFF != 0) and within RAM range (pa.0 < RAM_START || pa.0 >= RAM_END) before writing FreeBlock header.
VAL-ALLOC-007.details: PA validation is in the kernel's free_frames() behind #[cfg(not(test))]. The test uses model-based verification — duplicating the validation logic and checking all invalid input classes. This is sound because the model matches the kernel code exactly.

summary: Tested 7 assertions: all 7 passed. All 4 audited files (heap.rs, slab.rs, page_allocator.rs, memory_region.rs) have AUDIT comments with correct unsafe block counts and 6-category checklist applied. All unsafe blocks have SAFETY comments. OOM handling is documented in heap.rs AUDIT comment listing each allocation path with disposition. Dealloc routing tests verify address-based routing invariant with a greedy slab stub. PA validation tests verify misaligned, out-of-range, and boundary PAs are rejected. Full test suite: 974 passed, 0 failed. Kernel builds clean.
